h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: The bottom 4 labels this tile: quarter note gets one beat.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue following the tutor's steps and draw the bar line after the third quarter.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: The bottom 4 labels this tile: quarter note gets one beat.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: I think it is because `B` and `3` tell the place on the grid

scored.naiveTrajectory: After L1, the learner was expected to place friction on the left arrow without hesitation, following the card rule.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Friction stays on this left plastic arrow: the surface acts on the cart that way. The pencil mark is the cart’s travel mark, so it does not receive the force tile. For push, the tile has to go on the plastic arrow for the hand’s force, not just where the cart is moving.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: Learner completes the strip-gate task and extends the criterion to push, showing a new enacted device.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The old rule is not settling this because two marks are competing. Use this gate first: only marks visible above the strip can receive force labels.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: Tutor acknowledges the failure of the old route and introduces a physical gate to remove the conflicting mark.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Use this gate first: only marks visible above the strip can receive force labels.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The strip-gate is a precise, physically enacted constraint that directly resolves the learner's visual conflict, giving an immediate and transferable criterion.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No pivot learner turn exists to trigger adaptation.
